episode_meta.title: Mattia Peretti: Balancing AI Innovation with Journalistic Integrity

use_cases.description: Automated summaries added to articles, with editorial oversight and human in the loop, leading to increased time on page as summaries serve as entry points for readers
use_cases.outcome: Increased time on page; summaries act as entry points that encourage readers to read full articles rather than replacing them

use_cases.description: Repackaging content in different formats for different audiences, including AI-powered summaries and personalization
use_cases.outcome: Yahoo acquired Artifact to leverage its content repackaging and personalization capabilities

use_cases.description: Exploring innovative AI implementations across the organization with an internal working group spanning departments
use_cases.outcome: Published generative AI principles; not many public-facing projects yet but significant internal experimentation

use_cases.description: Agreement and licensing deal with OpenAI for French language content
Optimizing paywalls for subscription-based organizations using machine learning
Using AI to analyze and find information in huge datasets for investigative journalism
use_cases.outcome: Finding information in large datasets that would be impractical manually

use_cases.description: Building an internal AI literacy community via Microsoft Teams channel to facilitate knowledge sharing across a globally distributed organization
use_cases.outcome: Facilitated cross-organizational knowledge sharing on AI potential and risks across 100+ countries

use_cases.description: Journalism AI initiative working with teams on AI-powered summaries and making news archives accessible to journalists
use_cases.organization: Journalism AI (POLIS at LSE / Google News Initiative)
use_cases.outcome: Teams explored challenges in 2020 that remain relevant today, including AI summaries and archive search

key_claims.claim: The news industry has moved past the peak generative AI hype and into a more pragmatic phase at the beginning of 2024, with organizations focusing on strategy and real value rather than trying everything
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Automated article summaries increase time on page rather than replacing full article reads, because they serve as entry points that get people interested in reading more
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Challenges explored with AI in journalism in 2020 (AI-powered summaries, unlocking news archives) are still being explored today, showing these are ongoing evolving problems rather than ones with a single solution
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: AI literacy, experimentation/products, and guidelines/principles form a triangle where none can succeed without the other two
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Principles are more effective than prescriptive guidelines because technologies evolve so fast that detailed guidelines become outdated within a month
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Too-strict AI guidelines risk communicating to your team that management doesn't trust them to do the right thing
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Tech companies' AI development is a net negative for society as a whole
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

key_claims.claim: Tech companies should pay for access to news data because they are the richest organizations on the planet and it is a matter of fairness
key_claims.speaker: Mattia Peretti

challenges.description: Limitations of large language models remain significant and need to be considered when repackaging content, despite improvements
Organizations often approach AI adoption as a goal in itself rather than evaluating whether AI actually helps achieve specific organizational goals
Barrier to entry for AI adoption may be too high given an organization's resources and technical capacity, leading to wasted time
Knowledge sharing across newsrooms is a major challenge: some groups work with AI while others have no idea it is being used
AI risks are often relegated to an afterthought (e.g., last 5 minutes of a 50-minute webinar) rather than integrated into discussions from the start
AI guidelines risk becoming a performative exercise if not backed by real literacy and experimentation
Too-prescriptive guidelines become outdated within a month as technologies evolve rapidly
News organizations risk becoming hostage to tech company platforms and losing direct relationships with their audiences
Balancing the relationship with tech companies is extremely difficult: partnering risks naivety about profit motives, but complete detachment means missing real opportunities
Reliance on AI tools may atrophy cognitive capacities like memory, brainstorming, and independent information processing

quotes.text: I really feel it's like interesting this moment where We are beyond, as I was saying, the phase of let's try everything out. We're still trying things, but I see a healthy consolidation a little bit of the thinking into where is the real value. And ideally more value for the users, not just for us.
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

quotes.text: There is the AI literacy, there are the experiments in building products, and then everyone has been rushing to come up with guidelines and principles for generative AI and so on. Neither of these three things can exist and can have success without the other two, in my opinion.
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti
quotes.topic: The triangle of AI literacy, experimentation, and guidelines

quotes.text: I feel like the risk with putting to strict guidelines is to communicate in to your team that you don't trust them to know how to do the right thing. To really send the message then we as the management team or this AI working group, we are really afraid that you're gonna mess it up.
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

quotes.text: I think it's just very, very important that we're not naive about what are their goals. At the end of the day is these are for profit organizations. Their mission is profit. The mission is not the mission that we have as journalists.
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti

quotes.text: We need to do much more work in building that relationship and being independent from any tool, from any platform. We have seen it, you know, with tech com the social media, you know, putting down in their priorities news and so not producing it anymore.
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti
quotes.topic: Building direct audience relationships independent of platforms

quotes.text: We're gaining efficiency, we're doing some things faster. What are we losing? Like I use sometimes ChatGPT for helping me brainstorm an idea or start something new, and it is helpful sometimes. I get some useful feedback. But I also worry if I rely too much on it, what muscle in my brain I'm not exercising anymore?
quotes.speaker: Mattia Peretti
quotes.topic: Personal trade-offs of AI adoption and cognitive atrophy

recommendations.advice: Before adopting AI, spend more time asking 'why' — what are the organizational goals, and can AI actually help achieve them? Do not treat AI adoption as a value in itself.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Develop AI literacy, experimentation, and guidelines in parallel as a triangle — none can succeed without the other two. Do not take them step by step sequentially.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Write AI principles (the 'why') rather than prescriptive guidelines (the 'how'), because principles are closer to organizational values and do not become outdated as quickly as technology-specific guidelines.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Structure AI principles to address three distinct audiences: internal teams, readers, and society/industry at large.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Integrate discussion of AI risks from the very beginning of any training or literacy initiative rather than treating them as an afterthought.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti
recommendations.target_audience: AI literacy trainers and newsroom educators

recommendations.advice: Not everyone needs to become an AI expert, but everyone needs basic AI literacy. Combine organization-wide understanding with deeper expertise from a dedicated team or working group.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Build direct relationships with audiences that are not dependent on tech platforms, to avoid being hostage to platform priorities that can change at any time.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Do not be naive about tech companies' profit-driven motives in any partnership or collaboration, even while acknowledging the real opportunities they offer.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti

recommendations.advice: Facilitate internal knowledge sharing across departments and teams as a foundational step for AI literacy, especially in large distributed organizations.
recommendations.speaker: Mattia Peretti
recommendations.target_audience: Large media and media development organizations
